did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to automate the classification and counting of melanocytic naevi from 3D total body photography images. The CNN was trained on 57,742 lesion images to distinguish between naevi and non-naevi, and its performance was evaluated against gold standard counts by dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for automated naevus counting using 3D total body photography. The paper includes specific sections for Background, Objectives, Methods (describing training and test datasets from the Mind Your Moles study), Results (reporting sensitivity, specificity, and Cohen's kappa metrics), and Conclusion, which are characteristic of primary research studies rather than reviews.

title: Reproducible Naevus Counts Using 3D Total Body Photography and Convolutional Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study aimed to develop an automated, reproducible method for counting melanocytic naevi using 3D total body photography and convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to address the subjectivity of traditional counting. The CNN was trained on 57,742 lesion images from 82 subjects in the Mind Your Moles study and validated against a gold standard of in-clinic counts from 10 test subjects. Performance was evaluated at both the lesion and person levels using sensitivity, specificity, and Cohen’s kappa metrics. The algorithm achieved a sensitivity of 79% and specificity of 91% for lesions ≥2 mm, with substantial agreement (kappa 0.72) for lesions ≥5 mm. Person-level agreement between automated and expert counts was 70% when categorized by naevus count magnitude. The authors conclude that automated naevus counts show reasonable agreement with expert clinicians, offering a faster and more reproducible alternative for melanoma risk stratification.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the study; the CNN was trained and tested to classify lesions as 'naevi' versus 'non-naevi'. The study focused on counting melanocytic naevi for melanoma risk stratification.

The negative class in the binary classification task. This category included various benign skin conditions such as seborrhoeic keratoses, solar lentigines, and angiomas, which were excluded from the 'naevus' count.
Mentioned as a specific type of benign lesion that contributed to classification errors or mismatches between automated and expert counts, particularly in subjects with numerous lesions.
Listed as an example of 'other benign lesions' present in the test set that were not classified as naevi.
Listed as an example of 'other benign lesions' present in the test set that were not classified as naevi.
Mentioned as the disease for which naevus count is a risk factor. Some subjects had a history of melanoma, but melanoma itself was not a classification target of the CNN.
Mentioned in the context of subject history (50% reported history of keratinocyte cancer) and as a condition whose development might be better understood through naevus ecosystem analysis.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of lesions as 'naevus' or 'non-naevus'
all_task_types.details: The study developed a two-class Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifier to distinguish between melanocytic naevi and non-naevi (including seborrhoeic keratoses, solar lentigines, and angiomas) from 3D total body photography images. The model outputs probabilities for the 'Naevus' and 'Non-Naevus' classes. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and Cohen's kappa for lesion-level classification. While the ultimate goal was to count naevi (a regression-like aggregation task), the core AI task performed by the model is binary classification of individual lesions.

datasets_used.name: Mind Your Moles Study
datasets_used.description: A population-based cohort study in Queensland, Australia, where subjects were imaged using a VECTRA®WB360 system to create 3D total body photography avatars. Data was randomly selected from this study for both training and testing.

use.training: Used for training the CNN; 82 subjects provided 57,742 lesion images (≥2 mm) labeled by a senior dermatologist.
use.validation: Used for tuning CNN hyperparameters; 3 subjects provided 1,023 lesion images (including 248 naevi).
use.testing: Used for evaluating the CNN against gold standard in-clinic counts; 10 subjects provided 4,868 lesions (520 naevi, 4,348 non-naevi) labeled by three expert physicians.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess inter-rater agreement between automated counts and gold standard in-clinic counts at the lesion level for naevi ≥2 mm and ≥5 mm.
To evaluate the performance of the CNN classifier in distinguishing naevi from non-naevi at the lesion level for lesions ≥2 mm and ≥5 mm.
To provide uncertainty estimates for point estimates of Cohen's kappa, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value.
To compare total naevus counts (≥5 mm) between the automated algorithm and the in-clinic gold standard at the person level.
To assess agreement between automated and gold standard counts when categorized into magnitude categories (few, some, many) at the person level.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics including sensitivity, specificity, Cohen's kappa, overall accuracy, and 95% confidence intervals.

Mind Your Moles Study - Training Set.dataset_description: 82 subjects, 57,742 lesion images (≥2 mm), 5,106 naevi, 52,636 non-naevi
Mind Your Moles Study - Training Set.notes: The CNN was trained on this dataset. A validation subset of 3 subjects (1,023 lesions) was used to tune hyperparameters. Class imbalance was addressed by replicating 'Naevus' class images 8 times. Data augmentation included random rotations, flips, intensity, contrast, and colour adjustments.

Mind Your Moles Study - Test Set.dataset_description: 10 subjects (5 male, 5 female), 4,868 lesions (≥2 mm), 520 naevi, 4,348 non-naevi

person_level_results.agreement_category: 70% agreement between automated and gold standard counts when categorized by naevus count magnitude (few <20, some 20-50, many >50)
person_level_results.bland_altman_5mm: Median difference of 6-44 (IQR) for test set; when subjects with many seborrhoeic keratoses were excluded, 95% of automated counts ≥5 mm were within ±5 of in-clinic gold standard counts (79% within ±3)
person_level_results.correlation: 97% correlation between AI counts and gold standard test dataset

Mind Your Moles Study - Test Set.notes: The algorithm performed poorly on subjects with numerous seborrhoeic keratoses. In the test set, the algorithm overcalled counts in all 5 men. The test set consisted of subjects with a median age of 57 years, all of European descent.

architecture_used.pretraining: Network weights were initialised from a previously trained lesion analysis network.
architecture_used.input_size: Single perspective-corrected tile image of a lesion (generated by reprojecting original 2D views using calibrated 3D geometry information).

architecture_details.structure: Three blocks of two convolutional layers, a pooling layer, and Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation, followed by two fully connected layers.

training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Images in the 'Naevus' class were replicated 8 times.
training_details.data_augmentation: Random rotations, flips, intensity, contrast, and colour adjustments.

classification_or_output_head.output: Probability that the lesion belonged to the 'Naevus' or 'Non-Naevus' classes
classification_or_output_head.final_activation: Sigmoid (implied by sigmoid decay learning rate and binary probability output)

preprocessing_techniques: Reprojection of original 2D views using calibrated 3D geometry information to generate perspective-corrected tile images of lesions
Filtering to include only lesions ≥2 mm in diameter
Exclusion of lesions under underwear, on the scalp, or on the soles of the feet
Data augmentation via random rotations, flips, intensity adjustments, contrast adjustments, and colour adjustments
Class balancing by replicating 'Naevus' class images 8 times

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the full training data to reduce overfitting.
Applied to the full training data to reduce overfitting.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Class replication (Synthetic data generation for class balancing)
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images in the 'Naevus' class were replicated 8 times to address class imbalance.

conclusions: Automated naevus counts show reasonable agreement with expert clinician counts, demonstrating that reproducible counts are possible through the combination of 3D total body photography and convolutional neural networks (CNNs).

The automated algorithm provides a faster and more reproducible method for naevus counting compared to traditional in-person total body counts, which are time-intensive and subject to inter-rater variability.
The algorithm's performance was lower in subjects with numerous seborrhoeic keratoses, indicating a limitation in distinguishing naevi from other benign lesions in complex skin ecosystems.
Subjects with numerous seborrhoeic keratoses can be easily identified in the clinic and flagged for manual counts to mitigate the algorithm's limitations.
important_remarks: The test set was small (n=10 subjects), limiting the ability to evaluate performance across different subject characteristics such as photodamage level or sex.
The study population consisted exclusively of subjects of European descent with fair skin (Fitzpatrick types I-IV), limiting generalizability to darker skin types.
The algorithm performed poorly in subjects with numerous seborrhoeic keratoses, often overcalling naevus counts in these cases.
The algorithm overcalled counts in all 5 male subjects in the test set, though the authors note this could be due to chance.

Training labels were generated on-screen rather than via in-clinic dermatoscopy, whereas the test set gold standard was in-clinic counts, creating a potential modality mismatch for training data quality.
Class imbalance was addressed by replicating 'Naevus' class images 8 times rather than using more sophisticated sampling or loss weighting techniques.
The study did not report formal hypothesis testing (p-values), relying instead on point estimates and 95% confidence intervals for performance metrics.
